title: Stackelberg-Style Rival Response Features
summary: Public F1 strategy sources emphasize that undercut/overcut value depends on rival state, traffic, and warm-up.
rationale: Public F1 strategy sources emphasize that undercut/overcut value depends on rival state, traffic, and warm-up. Game-theoretic pit-stop research finds that neglecting competition materially worsens outcomes, so relative rival incentives are likely more informative than absolute tyre heuristics alone.
implementation_hint: Within each Year-Race-Lap, create nearest-ahead and nearest-behind rival features: tyre-age gap, lap-delta gap, same-compound flag, pit-count gap, and a leader/follower role indicator. Add compact undercut-threat and overcut-exposure scores rather than broad traffic feature sets.
expected_effect: Could add signal exactly where next-lap pit calls are tactical rather than purely degradation-driven.
risk: No explicit time-gap column means rival-response proxies may be noisy at dense midfield laps.
